faculty_information.status_rank_title: Full-Time Assistant Professor of Computer Science
faculty_information.department_college: Department of Computer Science, College of Engineering and Computing
faculty_information.teaching_discipline: Computer Science (Software Engineering and Data Science)
faculty_information.course_assignments_or_level_of_instructi:
Primary assignment at the undergraduate and graduate levels.

Undergraduate Courses (Fall 2025 – Spring 2026):
1. CS 2310 – Data Structures and Algorithms (Undergraduate, sophomore/junior level)
   - Sections: 2 sections per semester (Fall and Spring)
   - Approximate enrollment: 30–35 students per section
   - Emphasis on algorithmic thinking, complexity analysis, and implementation in Python and Java.

2. CS 3415 – Software Engineering I (Undergraduate, junior level)
   - Sections: 1 section in Fall 2025
   - Approximate enrollment: 35–40 students
   - Focus on software development life cycle, requirements engineering, version control, unit testing, and team-based semester-long project.

3. CS 4890 – Capstone Project in Computer Science (Undergraduate, senior level)
   - Sections: 1 section in Spring 2026
   - Approximate enrollment: 18–22 students
   - Supervision of student teams designing and implementing full-stack applications with an industry or community partner.

Graduate Courses (Master’s level):
4. CS 5312 – Advanced Data Structures and Algorithm Design (Graduate)
   - Offered: Fall 2025
   - Approximate enrollment: 18–22 students
   - Focus on advanced topics such as graph algorithms, randomized algorithms, amortized analysis, and algorithm design paradigms.

5. CS 5720 – Applied Machine Learning and Data Science (Graduate)
   - Offered: Spring 2026
   - Approximate enrollment: 20–25 students
   - Emphasis on supervised and unsupervised learning, model evaluation, data preprocessing, and deployment of ML models using Python (scikit-learn, pandas, PyTorch).

Additional Responsibilities:
- Regular supervision of 3–5 undergraduate research assistants per academic year.
- Service course guest lectures in introductory programming for non-majors (as requested by department).

Syllabi for each listed course are attached separately in the official submission.

qualifications.detailed_description_of_qualifications:
Overview:
Dr. Elena Marisol Ruiz is academically and professionally qualified to teach undergraduate and graduate courses in Computer Science, with a specialization in software engineering and data science. She meets and exceeds SACSCOC credentialing guidelines by holding a terminal degree (Ph.D. in Computer Science) in the teaching discipline and by maintaining an active record of research, industry collaboration, and continuous professional development.

Academic Preparation:
1. Terminal Degree (Ph.D. in Computer Science, UIUC, 2019):
   - The Ph.D. is directly aligned with the teaching discipline of Computer Science and specifically supports her assigned courses in software engineering, data structures and algorithms, and applied machine learning.
   - Key coursework included: Advanced Algorithms, Machine Learning, Data Mining, Software Architecture, Program Analysis, and Statistical Learning Theory.
   - Her dissertation, "Scalable and Interpretable Machine Learning Methods for Large-Scale Software Systems," involved designing algorithms and tools that analyze millions of lines of source code and software logs to predict defects and performance regressions. This research directly informs her teaching of CS 5312 (Advanced Data Structures and Algorithm Design) and CS 5720 (Applied Machine Learning and Data Science), where she uses anonymized case studies taken from her experiments.

2. Master’s Degree (M.S. in Computer Science, UT Austin, 2013):
   - Focused on software engineering and automated testing, providing a strong foundation for CS 3415 (Software Engineering I) and CS 4890 (Capstone Project in Computer Science).
   - Her master’s thesis on automated test generation for web applications exposed her to real-world software reliability and quality assurance challenges, which she transforms into hands-on lab assignments. For example, she has students create automated test suites for a simplified e-commerce application, mirroring the techniques she evaluated in her thesis.

3. Bachelor’s Degree (B.S. in Computer Engineering, SDSU, 2011):
   - The combination of computer engineering and a minor in applied mathematics ensures she has the breadth of technical knowledge and mathematical rigor to teach foundational courses in algorithms and data structures.
   - Her senior project on wireless sensor networks is periodically used as an example in class when discussing distributed systems and real-time constraints.

Related Work Experience (Professional and Industry):
1. Senior Machine Learning Engineer (Part-Time Consultant)
   Organization: BlueWave Analytics, Inc. (Austin, TX – remote)
   Dates: 2020-06 to Present
   - Role: Advises on the design and deployment of predictive models for large-scale log data and customer behavior. Works approximately 5–8 hours per week during the academic year and 10–15 hours per week during summers.
   - Responsibilities: Model selection, feature engineering, performance evaluation, and MLOps best practices.
   - Relevance to Teaching: Uses sanitized, de-identified datasets from BlueWave as case studies in her Applied Machine Learning course, allowing students to work with realistic, messy data. She also introduces industry-standard tools (e.g., Docker, GitHub Actions, and CI/CD pipelines) in CS 3415 and CS 4890 to mirror her consultative work.

2. Software Engineer II (Full-Time)
   Organization: Atlassian (Austin, TX)
   Dates: 2014-01 to 2014-07 (prior to starting Ph.D.)
   - Responsibilities: Worked on development of internal tools for performance monitoring and test automation for a large-scale SaaS product.
   - Relevance to Teaching: This experience informs her emphasis on code quality, scalable architectures, and real-world constraints in software engineering courses. She frequently shares concrete anecdotes, such as debugging memory leaks in production and coordinating with distributed teams across time zones.

3. Undergraduate Research Assistant and Internships (During B.S. at SDSU):
   - Undergraduate Research Assistant in Embedded Systems Lab (2010-2011), focusing on sensor networks.
   - Software Engineering Intern at Qualcomm (Summer 2010), working on driver testing and automation scripts.
   - These early experiences help her advise students on internships and undergraduate research pathways.

Professional Licensure and Certifications:
Although formal licensure is not typically required in Computer Science, Dr. Ruiz maintains several industry-recognized certifications that keep her current with tools and best practices used in modern software and data science:

1. AWS Certified Solutions Architect – Associate
   Issuing Organization: Amazon Web Services
   Date Awarded: 2021-03-10
   - Demonstrates her ability to design and deploy scalable applications on cloud infrastructure, which she leverages in CS 4890 Capstone when guiding students to deploy their projects in a cloud environment.

2. Google Professional Data Engineer Certification
   Issuing Organization: Google Cloud
   Date Awarded: 2022-07-18
   - Validates her expertise in building and maintaining data processing systems, machine learning pipelines, and data storage solutions. This directly supports her teaching in CS 5720, where students build and evaluate ML pipelines on real datasets.

3. Certified ScrumMaster (CSM)
   Issuing Organization: Scrum Alliance
   Date Awarded: 2018-11-05
   - She actively incorporates Scrum and agile principles into CS 3415 (Software Engineering I) and CS 4890 (Capstone). Students work in agile teams, maintain sprint backlogs, and conduct retrospectives, aligning course practices with standard industry workflows.

Honors and Awards:
1. College of Engineering and Computing Outstanding Teaching Award (Early Career)
   Institution: [Institution Name Redacted for Simulation], College of Engineering and Computing
   Date: 2024-04-22
   - Awarded based on student evaluations, peer observations, and evidence of innovative course design.

2. UIUC Graduate College Dissertation Completion Fellowship
   Institution: University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign
   Date: 2018-08 to 2019-05
   - Competitive fellowship that supported the completion of her Ph.D. research in scalable machine learning.

3. Best Paper Award – International Conference on Software Analytics (ICSA)
   Paper: "Interpretable Deep Learning Models for Predicting Software Defects from Large-Scale Code Repositories"
   Date: 2020-09-15
   - This paper is used as a reading assignment in her graduate machine learning course, where students critically analyze the trade-offs between model accuracy and interpretability.

4. SDSU College of Engineering Outstanding Graduating Senior in Computer Engineering
   Institution: San Diego State University
   Date: 2011-05-10

Continuous Documented Excellence in Teaching:
1. Student Evaluations:
   - Over the last three academic years (2022–2025), her average overall teaching effectiveness score has been between 4.5 and 4.8 out of 5.0 across all courses.
   - Students consistently praise her clarity of explanations, responsiveness to questions (particularly via online discussion boards and office hours), and the real-world relevance of assignments.

2. Peer Observations and Teaching Portfolio:
   - Departmental peer observations (2022 and 2024) note her effective use of active learning strategies, such as think-pair-share activities, live coding sessions, and structured problem-solving workshops.
   - Her teaching portfolio includes detailed syllabi, sample assignments, grading rubrics, and reflective statements documenting iterative improvements. For example, she redesigned Data Structures and Algorithms to include weekly formative quizzes and short in-class coding challenges to improve student retention of core concepts.

3. Professional Development in Pedagogy:
   - Completed a Graduate Certificate in College Teaching and Learning in STEM at UIUC (2018), which covered course design, inclusive teaching practices, and assessment strategies.
   - Regularly attends the institution’s annual Teaching and Learning Conference and has presented on topics such as "Using Real-World Datasets to Motivate Algorithms" and "Reducing Performance Gaps in CS1 Through Structured Peer Instruction."

Additional Demonstrated Competencies and Achievements:
1. Curriculum Development:
   - Led the redesign of CS 3415 (Software Engineering I) in 2023 to introduce a more robust project-based structure, integrating GitHub Classroom for assignment distribution and continuous integration workflows.
   - Co-developed a new elective, "Introduction to Responsible AI and Software Ethics," which is under review by the curriculum committee. This course will draw on her research in interpretable machine learning and ethical considerations in data use.

2. Research and Scholarly Activity:
   - Published 8 peer-reviewed journal articles and 12 conference papers in venues related to software engineering, machine learning, and software analytics.
   - Currently serves as a reviewer for journals such as Empirical Software Engineering and IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering.
   - Actively involves undergraduates and master’s students in research projects, several of whom have co-authored publications or presented posters at regional conferences.

3. Service and Mentoring:
   - Faculty advisor for the Women in Computing student organization at the institution, where she mentors students on navigating internships, research opportunities, and graduate school applications.
   - Coordinates an annual "Code for Community" hackathon that pairs student teams with local non-profits to develop simple web or data visualization tools, reinforcing the applied skills taught in her courses.

4. Technical Competencies:
   - Programming Languages: Python, Java, C++, JavaScript/TypeScript, SQL.
   - Tools and Platforms: Git/GitHub, Docker, AWS, Google Cloud Platform, Kubernetes (basic), Jupyter Notebooks, VS Code, IntelliJ IDEA.
   - ML/Data Tools: scikit-learn, PyTorch (introductory), TensorFlow (basic), pandas, NumPy, matplotlib, Plotly.
   - These competencies allow her to provide up-to-date, practical instruction and to guide students in selecting appropriate technologies for their projects.

Summary Justification for Teaching Assignments:
- CS 2310 – Data Structures and Algorithms: Fully supported by her B.S., M.S., and Ph.D. coursework and research in algorithms and software systems. She has taught this course or its equivalent for four consecutive years, continuously refining assignments to emphasize both theoretical understanding and practical implementation.

- CS 3415 – Software Engineering I and CS 4890 – Capstone Project: Supported by her M.S. specialization in software engineering, her industry experience at Atlassian, ongoing consulting work, and her Certified ScrumMaster credential. She integrates agile methodologies, version control, code review practices, and automated testing into these courses.

- CS 5312 – Advanced Data Structures and Algorithm Design and CS 5720 – Applied Machine Learning and Data Science: Supported by her Ph.D. research in scalable machine learning and algorithm design, as well as her ongoing applied work as a machine learning consultant. She regularly updates the course content to reflect new developments in machine learning libraries, techniques for handling large datasets, and best practices for model evaluation.

Collectively, Dr. Ruiz’s advanced degrees in Computer Science, her active research agenda, her relevant industry and consulting experience, and her documented excellence in teaching clearly demonstrate that she is well qualified for her assigned teaching responsibilities in Computer Science at both the undergraduate and graduate levels.
